content.text: OpenTelemetry (OTel) is a vendor-neutral, CNCF-graduated observability standard that defines a data model (traces, metrics, logs), a wire protocol (OTLP), and an SDK for every major language. When you instrument an agent with OTel directly, you gain the ability to send traces to any compatible backend — Jaeger, Grafana Tempo, Honeycomb, Arize Phoenix, or a self-hosted collector — without rewriting instrumentation code. The AI-specific frameworks (LangSmith, Langfuse, Phoenix) are all built on top of OTel or accept OTLP; understanding the layer beneath them lets you debug problems those wrappers hide, mix agent spans with infrastructure spans in the same trace, and switch backends without touching application code.

attrs.alt: OpenTelemetry agent pipeline: agent code → OTel SDK → optional Collector → Jaeger / Tempo / Phoenix

content.text: — a directed tree of spans sharing a trace_id. One user request → one trace.
— a named, timed unit of work. Each span knows its parent_span_id, so the tree can be reconstructed from a flat list.
— the factory for Tracer objects. You configure exporters and processors here, once, at startup.
— hooks that fire on span start and end. BatchSpanProcessor batches spans and exports asynchronously; SimpleSpanProcessor exports synchronously (useful in tests, slow in production).
— key-value metadata attached to every span from a process: service.name, service.version, deployment.environment.
— the active span is stored in a thread-local (or asyncio task-local) context. Child spans automatically inherit the parent without explicit ID passing.

Configure the provider once at application startup. Attach a Resource for service identity and a BatchSpanProcessor that exports to your backend over OTLP/HTTP. After calling set_tracer_provider(), any code in the same process can obtain a Tracer with get_tracer() and start spans immediately.

from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.http.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
from opentelemetry.sdk.resources import Resource

# Resource attributes appear on every span from this process
resource = Resource.create({
    "service.name": "support-agent",
    "service.version": "2.4.0",
    "deployment.environment": "production",
})

# OTLP/HTTP exporter — set endpoint to your backend
exporter = OTLPSpanExporter(
    endpoint="http://localhost:4318/v1/traces",  # OTel Collector or Phoenix
)

provider = TracerProvider(resource=resource)
provider.add_span_processor(BatchSpanProcessor(exporter))

# Register globally — get_tracer() anywhere in this process uses this provider
trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)
For tests, swap BatchSpanProcessor for SimpleSpanProcessor with an InMemorySpanExporter so you can assert on spans synchronously without network calls or latency.

from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export.in_memory_span_exporter import InMemorySpanExporter
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import SimpleSpanProcessor

span_exporter = InMemorySpanExporter()
provider = TracerProvider(resource=resource)
provider.add_span_processor(SimpleSpanProcessor(span_exporter))
trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)

# After running your agent:
spans = span_exporter.get_finished_spans()
llm_spans = [s for s in spans if s.name == "llm_call"]
assert len(llm_spans) >= 1
assert llm_spans[0].attributes["gen_ai.request.model"] == "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"

Get a module-level Tracer with get_tracer(__name__). Use start_as_current_span as a context manager — the span starts when you enter the with block and ends (and is exported) when you leave. Any span created inside the block automatically becomes a child of the outer span through OTel's context propagation.

from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.trace import StatusCode

tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)

def run_agent_turn(session_id: str, user_message: str) -> str:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("agent_turn") as span:
        # Attributes are queryable in the backend UI
        span.set_attribute("session.id", session_id)
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.operation.name", "agent")
        span.set_attribute("input.value", user_message)

        try:
            response = _do_agent_loop(user_message)
            span.set_attribute("output.value", response)
            span.set_status(StatusCode.OK)
            return response
        except Exception as exc:
            span.record_exception(exc)            # attaches exception + stack trace
            span.set_status(StatusCode.ERROR, str(exc))
            raise

def _call_llm(prompt: str, model: str) -> str:
    # Child span — automatically parented to agent_turn via context
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("llm_call") as span:
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.system", "anthropic")
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.request.model", model)
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.operation.name", "chat")

        response = _invoke_anthropic(prompt, model)

        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.usage.input_tokens", response.usage.input_tokens)
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.usage.output_tokens", response.usage.output_tokens)
        return response.content[0].text

OpenTelemetry's Gen AI semantic conventions define the attribute names that backends, dashboards, and tooling agree to interpret. Using the standard names means your spans render correctly in Phoenix, Grafana, and any OTel-aware tool without custom parsers. The conventions are in the opentelemetry-semantic-conventions package under semconv.trace.
Model identifier sent in the request
Model actually used (may differ from request)

Span events are timestamped log-like records attached to an in-progress span. They are cheaper than child spans and are ideal for things that happen within a span's lifetime without needing their own start/end times — receiving a streaming chunk, emitting a retry, hitting a cache.

def call_llm_streaming(prompt: str) -> str:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("llm_call_stream") as span:
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.operation.name", "chat")
        chunks = []
        for i, chunk in enumerate(stream_anthropic(prompt)):
            # One event per chunk — visible in the trace waterfall as tick marks
            span.add_event(
                "stream_chunk",
                attributes={"chunk_index": i, "delta_tokens": len(chunk.split())},
            )
            chunks.append(chunk)
        full_text = "".join(chunks)
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.usage.output_tokens", len(full_text.split()))
        return full_text

Span links connect spans from different traces — for example, when an async job is triggered from a user request and you want to reference the originating trace from the job's trace. You pass a SpanContext (trace_id + span_id) as a link rather than a parent.

from opentelemetry.trace import Link, NonRecordingSpan, SpanContext, TraceFlags

def process_async_job(job_id: str, originating_trace_id: int, originating_span_id: int):
    # Link this job's trace back to the request that triggered it
    origin_ctx = SpanContext(
        trace_id=originating_trace_id,
        span_id=originating_span_id,
        is_remote=True,
        trace_flags=TraceFlags(TraceFlags.SAMPLED),
    )
    link = Link(context=origin_ctx, attributes={"link.type": "triggered_by"})

    with tracer.start_as_current_span("async_job", links=[link]) as span:
        span.set_attribute("job.id", job_id)
        _run_job(job_id)
Context Propagation Across Async and Threads

OTel's context is stored per asyncio task (not per thread) when you use async code, so the active span flows correctly into awaited coroutines automatically. For threads, you must attach the context manually because threads do not inherit the parent task's context.

import asyncio
from opentelemetry import context, trace
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)

# Async: context propagates automatically
async def run_async_turn(message: str) -> str:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("agent_turn") as span:     # sets active span
        span.set_attribute("input.value", message)
        result = await call_llm_async(message)                   # child span inside await
        return result

async def call_llm_async(prompt: str) -> str:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("llm_call") as span:      # automatically parented
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.operation.name", "chat")
        return await _invoke_async(prompt)

# Threads: snapshot and attach context explicitly
def run_tool_in_thread(tool_name: str, query: str, ctx) -> str:
    token = context.attach(ctx)           # attach the parent's context to this thread
    try:
        with tracer.start_as_current_span("tool_call") as span:
            span.set_attribute("tool.name", tool_name)
            return _invoke_tool(tool_name, query)
    finally:
        context.detach(token)             # always detach to avoid leaks

def dispatch_tools_parallel(tools: list[dict]) -> list[str]:
    ctx = context.get_current()           # snapshot context in parent thread
    with ThreadPoolExecutor() as pool:
        futures = [
            pool.submit(run_tool_in_thread, t["name"], t["query"], ctx)
            for t in tools
        ]
        return [f.result() for f in futures]

Baggage is a key-value store that propagates with the context across in-process and across-process boundaries (via W3C Baggage HTTP headers). Use it to carry values like user_id or tenant_id that you want available deep in the call stack without threading them through every function signature. Baggage is separate from span attributes — it does not appear in the span itself, but you can read it at any point to stamp span attributes.

from opentelemetry import baggage, context

def handle_request(user_id: str, tenant: str, message: str) -> str:
    # Stamp baggage at the entry point
    ctx = baggage.set_baggage("user.id", user_id)
    ctx = baggage.set_baggage("tenant", tenant, context=ctx)
    token = context.attach(ctx)
    try:
        return _process(message)
    finally:
        context.detach(token)

def _process(message: str) -> str:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("llm_call") as span:
        # Read baggage anywhere without passing it explicitly
        uid = baggage.get_baggage("user.id")
        tenant = baggage.get_baggage("tenant")
        span.set_attribute("user.id", uid or "")
        span.set_attribute("tenant", tenant or "")
        return _invoke_llm(message)

Sampling controls what fraction of traces are recorded. In production, recording every span from a high-throughput agent is expensive; sampling lets you keep enough data for meaningful analysis without the full cost.
Disable tracing in tests without removing code
Records p fraction of traces deterministically

from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.sampling import TraceIdRatioBased, ParentBased

# Sample 10% of traces in production
sampler = ParentBased(root=TraceIdRatioBased(0.1))

provider = TracerProvider(
    resource=resource,
    sampler=sampler,
)
provider.add_span_processor(BatchSpanProcessor(exporter))
trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)

The OTel Collector is an optional but useful proxy that sits between your application and your backends. It lets you change where traces go (add Grafana Tempo without touching application code), filter out noisy spans, tail-sample (make sampling decisions after seeing the full trace), and batch-compress spans for efficiency. You configure it with a YAML file and run it as a Docker container.

# otel-collector-config.yaml
receivers:
  otlp:
    protocols:
      http:
        endpoint: 0.0.0.0:4318

processors:
  batch:
    timeout: 5s
    send_batch_size: 512
  filter/drop-health:
    spans:
      exclude:
        match_type: strict
        span_names: ["health_check"]

exporters:
  otlp/jaeger:
    endpoint: jaeger:4317
    tls:
      insecure: true
  otlp/phoenix:
    endpoint: phoenix:4317
    tls:
      insecure: true

service:
  pipelines:
    traces:
      receivers: [otlp]
      processors: [filter/drop-health, batch]
      exporters: [otlp/jaeger, otlp/phoenix]

The following shows a complete two-turn agent with TracerProvider setup, root span, nested LLM and tool spans, and proper error handling. All OTel setup is in one place (setup_tracing); agent code only imports get_tracer and start_as_current_span.

import anthropic
from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.http.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
from opentelemetry.sdk.resources import Resource
from opentelemetry.trace import StatusCode

def setup_tracing(service: str, endpoint: str) -> None:
    resource = Resource.create({"service.name": service})
    exporter = OTLPSpanExporter(endpoint=endpoint)
    provider = TracerProvider(resource=resource)
    provider.add_span_processor(BatchSpanProcessor(exporter))
    trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)

setup_tracing("support-agent", "http://localhost:4318/v1/traces")
tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)
llm = anthropic.Anthropic()

TOOLS = [
    {"name": "web_search", "description": "Search the web",
     "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"query": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["query"]}}
]

def call_tool(name: str, args: dict) -> str:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("tool_call") as span:
        span.set_attribute("tool.name", name)
        span.set_attribute("input.value", str(args))
        if name == "web_search":
            result = f"[search result for: {args['query']}]"
        else:
            result = "unknown tool"
        span.set_attribute("output.value", result)
        return result

def agent_turn(session_id: str, messages: list) -> str:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("agent_turn") as root:
        root.set_attribute("session.id", session_id)
        root.set_attribute("gen_ai.operation.name", "agent")
        try:
            while True:
                with tracer.start_as_current_span("llm_call") as llm_span:
                    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.system", "anthropic")
                    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.request.model", "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")
                    response = llm.messages.create(
                        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
                        max_tokens=1024,
                        tools=TOOLS,
                        messages=messages,
                    )
                    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.usage.input_tokens", response.usage.input_tokens)
                    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.usage.output_tokens", response.usage.output_tokens)
                    llm_span.set_attribute("gen_ai.response.finish_reasons", [response.stop_reason])

                if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
                    text = next(b.text for b in response.content if hasattr(b, "text"))
                    root.set_attribute("output.value", text)
                    root.set_status(StatusCode.OK)
                    return text

                # Handle tool use
                messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})
                tool_results = []
                for block in response.content:
                    if block.type == "tool_use":
                        result = call_tool(block.name, block.input)
                        tool_results.append({
                            "type": "tool_result",
                            "tool_use_id": block.id,
                            "content": result,
                        })
                messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})

        except Exception as exc:
            root.record_exception(exc)
            root.set_status(StatusCode.ERROR, str(exc))
            raise

if __name__ == "__main__":
    reply = agent_turn(
        session_id="sess-001",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is OpenTelemetry?"}],
    )
    print(reply)
    # Flush pending spans before exit
    trace.get_tracer_provider().shutdown()

OpenTelemetry gives you a vendor-neutral foundation for agent observability. You configure a TracerProvider once with a Resource (service identity), a SpanProcessor (batch for production, in-memory for tests), and an OTLP exporter pointing at any compatible backend. In agent code, get_tracer(__name__) and start_as_current_span handle parent-child relationships automatically through context propagation — no manual ID threading required. Span events record timestamped occurrences within a span, span links connect spans across traces, and baggage carries cross-cutting values without polluting function signatures. The OTel Collector (optional) decouples your code from specific backends and handles filtering, batching, and fan-out to multiple destinations. The Gen AI semantic conventions (gen_ai.system, gen_ai.request.model, gen_ai.usage.*) ensure your LLM spans render correctly in any OTel-aware observability tool.
